In my view, in the field of video games, especially online multiplayer, I consider that many companies invest more resources in how to keep players endowed with mediocre games than in creating quality products.

paragraphs.text: In my view, in the field of video games, especially online multiplayer, I consider that many companies invest more resources in how to keep players endowed with mediocre games than in creating quality products. Patents such as US20160005270A1, US9993735B2, US10610786B2, US10751629B2 and US20170259178A1 describe matchmaking systems designed to manipulate the emotional state of the player. For example, they paired players wearing a bad racha with others who have a winning racha, putting them in the same team, so that the player in bad racha can win (thus alleviating his frustration) and that of good racha is more likely to lose (creating frustration). All of this is in order to keep both stranded, increase play hours, improve retention and encourage the purchase of microtransactions. Another example of manipulation is in many competitive games with ranks, such as in Marvel Rivals, where the ranges are artificially reset every new season. This leads to high level players (e.g. Master ranks) being temporarily degraded to low ranges (Gold or even Plata), while average players (Gold) fall into even lower ranges (Bronze). The player Master can then wait for gold players to return to gold to face easier consignments, taking advantage of their advantage to rise quickly, while players with a lower level of skill suffer frustrating defeats. This system creates an artificially volatile and toxic environment, which benefits only the company’s retention indicators. The aim seems to be to keep players active and frustrated equally, creating a false sense of progress and forcing them to play more lots. These practices generate a toxic and artificial experience, far removed from what should be a fair or fun competition. Moreover, algorithms that manipulate the player’s experience do so without informing or seeking consent. Emotionally manipulating the player to maximise economic benefits is an abusive practice that should be illegal, especially when it affects rankings, tournaments, rewards and classification systems. On the other hand, many companies want to reserve the right to close games that users have bought. In my case, I bought Overwatch 1, but Blizzard closed the game and replaced it with Overwatch 2, which has a different play and a different business model. I have no access to what I bought, no choice to continue playing, nor a possibility to complain. Such unilateral closure should be prohibited. If a company decides to abandon a product, it should at least allow the self-hosting of private servers so that the game community can keep it alive. There is already a European Citizens’ Initiative with more than one million signatures https://eci.ec.europa.eu/045/public. I am also concerned about the psychological impact of these mechanics on players, especially minors: they induce artificial frustration, emotional dependence and compulsive consumption. They are practices that rotate or directly cross the line of psychological manipulation for commercial purposes. In the light of the above, I would like to ask that: The use of manipulative matchmaking algorithms such as those described in the above mentioned patents is prohibited, at least in competitive games. The right of consumers to access products they have purchased indefinitely is regulated. The negative psychological impact of these practices is recognised and legislated accordingly. Thank you for your time.
